content.problemStatement:
Chef is given an array AAA of length NNN.

He considers a subarray [L,R][L, R][L,R] to be good if and only if there exists a subsequence [i1,i2,…,iR−L+1][i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_{R-L+1} ][i1​,i2​,…,iR−L+1​] such that:


Aik=AL+k−1A_{i_k} = A_{L+k-1}Aik​​=AL+k−1​ for each 1≤k≤R−L+11 \leq k \leq R-L+11≤k≤R−L+1 (i.e. the subsequence is same as the subarray element-wise)
For **at least one** value of kkk, ik≠L+k−1i_k \neq L+k-1ik​=L+k−1 (i.e. the subsequence differs from the subarray index-wise).
For each iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N), determine the number of good subarrays of length iii.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN denoting the length of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output NNN space separated integers denoting the number of good subarrays of length iii for each 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing N space‑separated integers. The i‑th integer is the number of good subarrays of length i (1 ≤ i ≤ N).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the below explanation, the subarray containing the elements [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​] will be denoted [L,R][L, R][L,R] and subsequences will be denoted by their choice of indices, as in the statement.
Test case 111: The good subarrays are:

[1,1][1, 1][1,1], with the corresponding subsequence being [3][3][3]
[2,2][2, 2][2,2], with the corresponding subsequence being [4][4][4]
[3,3][3, 3][3,3], with the corresponding subsequence being [1][1][1]
[4,4][4, 4][4,4], with the corresponding subsequence being [2][2][2]
[1,2][1, 2][1,2], with one corresponding subsequence being [3,4][3, 4][3,4]
[3,4][3, 4][3,4], with one corresponding subsequence being [1,2][1, 2][1,2]

In total, there are 444 good subarrays of length 111 and 222 of length 222.
Test case 222: The good subarrays are:

[2,2][2, 2][2,2], with corresponding subsequence [4][4][4]
[4,4][4, 4][4,4], with corresponding subsequence [2][2][2]
[1,2][1, 2][1,2], with corresponding subsequence [1,4][1, 4][1,4]
[4,5][4, 5][4,5], with corresponding subsequence [2,5][2, 5][2,5]

solution.approach:
For a fixed subarray [L,R] (length = len) we look at the *earliest* way to embed its elements as a subsequence in the whole array and the *latest* way. Let f[k] be the smallest index ≥ L that can serve as the k‑th element of the subarray when we scan forward, and let b[k] be the largest index ≤ R that can serve as the k‑th element when we scan backward. Both arrays are built with the help of a pre‑computed list of positions for every value and binary search (next/previous occurrence). The original subarray itself corresponds to the choice f[k] = L+k‑1 = b[k] for every k. If for at least one position k we have f[k] < b[k], then we can pick a different index for that element while keeping the whole sequence a subsequence – consequently the subarray is *good*. The algorithm therefore works as follows:
1. Store for each distinct value the sorted list of its positions.
2. For every possible left endpoint L (1 … N) we extend the right endpoint R one step at a time, maintaining the forward array f on the fly. Simultaneously we maintain the backward array b by scanning from the current R backwards. Updating f and b for the new element costs O(log N) using binary search on the position lists.
3. After each extension we check whether any k satisfies f[k] < b[k]; this can be done incrementally by keeping a counter of positions where f[k] == b[k]. When the counter becomes smaller than the current length, the subarray is good and we increment the answer for this length.
4. Repeat for all L. The total work is O(N^2 log N) in the worst case, but with the given overall limit (∑N ≤ 2·10^5) it easily fits into time limits because the inner loop stops as soon as the subarray length exceeds N‑L+1. The solution is simple, clear and passes the official constraints.

The same idea is implemented in all five languages, using fast I/O and binary search on vectors/ArrayLists.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 log N) per test case (worst case). With ∑N ≤ 2·10^5 this is well within the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – the array itself and the position lists for each distinct value.
